title: Precision Under Pressure
summary: A performance-led campaign film built around impact edits, restrained VFX, and celebrity presence that never feels manufactured.
description: I approached the ONE8 film like a controlled pressure chamber. The brief had star power baked into it, but I wanted the craft to feel disciplined instead of noisy. The camera language had to carry confidence before the effects arrived.

My role was to direct the rhythm and make the post work invisible. Every light pass, transition, and composite existed to reinforce Virat's presence instead of competing with it. When the VFX are doing their job, the viewer only feels momentum.

title: Velocity Fragments
summary: An energy-forward motion piece where speed came from visual timing, texture, and selective digital augmentation.
description: Nike projects punish hesitation. The frame either moves with conviction or it dies. I built the piece around aggressive pacing and visual texture, then used VFX only where the impact curve needed more acceleration.
The fun part was balancing polish with grit. I wanted the film to feel premium without scrubbing away the friction that makes movement believable.

title: Silicon Heat
summary: A CGI-led brand story focused on engineered surfaces, thermal light, and hardware treated like cinema.
description: With AMD, I was interested in translating performance into atmosphere. Instead of explaining the technology literally, I leaned into materiality: glow behavior, edge reflections, heat signatures, and a camera path that felt precise instead of flashy.
CGI works best for me when it behaves like production design. The objective is not to show that something was rendered. It is to make an invisible system feel tangible.

title: Object Memory
summary: A tactile 3D narrative for product moments, designed to make software feel physical and intimate.
description: Spotify has a rare challenge: the product lives in software, but the audience experiences it emotionally. I built a 3D visual language that translated interface actions into objects, surfaces, and motion cues you can almost touch.
What mattered most was restraint. The shapes had to feel iconic, not ornamental. Every movement was tuned to sound like a visual beat.

title: Retail in Motion
summary: A modular motion system for retail visuals that translated everyday objects into scalable digital assets.
description: IKEA work demands clarity before style. The solution had to survive across formats, teams, and timelines, so I built a modular visual system where the objects stayed familiar but the motion added precision and warmth.
I think of projects like this as infrastructure. If the logic is strong, the outputs can evolve without the identity fragmenting.

title: Altitude Language
summary: A motion-led premium brand film built around elegance, travel psychology, and invisible digital finishing.
description: Luxury communication falls apart the moment it tries too hard. For Qatar Airways, I focused on tension, negative space, and motion that moved with confidence rather than speed.
The VFX layer was there to remove friction, extend scale, and refine the transitions between ideas. The ambition was a film that felt expensive because it felt controlled.

title: Future Signals
summary: A speculative visual identity for a Web3 product designed to feel credible, cinematic, and ahead of the category.
description: Web3 branding often mistakes chaos for futurism. I wanted Space to feel composed, system-driven, and visually literate. That meant building a language of signals, grids, and motion cues that felt more like mission control than marketing.
I used AI-assisted ideation where it accelerated exploration, then rebuilt the final frames with tighter art direction and stronger design logic.

title: The Blackout
summary: A narrative short built around silence, isolation, and image-first storytelling.
description: The Blackout is where I let instinct lead more than systems. It came from a need to make something intimate, something that could hold tension without over-explaining itself.
Writing and directing it reminded me that the most powerful visual decisions are often subtraction. Remove the unnecessary, and the frame starts saying more.

title: Quiet Luxury for Web3
summary: A brand world for OKU that traded crypto noise for premium restraint and motion clarity.
description: OKU needed a visual identity that felt informed by finance and fashion, not meme culture. I built a slower, more deliberate motion system with clean geometry and enough texture to keep it human.
This kind of work is less about style frames and more about trust. Every design decision either raises credibility or destroys it.

title: Burst Systems
summary: A fast-moving visual toolkit for a Web3 launch that needed coherence across experimental outputs.
description: Burst was messy in the best possible way: ambitious, fast, and hungry for a visual system that could stretch across launches without losing itself. I treated the identity like a set of rules instead of a set of assets.
That is usually where I do my best work. I am less interested in one beautiful frame than in building a repeatable visual language that keeps delivering after the first drop.
